reason: The paper does use "decrease of node impurity" for RF and explicitly defends this choice over permutation importance because SNPs share identical scale/cardinality (0/1/2) and are highly correlated (LD), which is a reasoned methodological point rather than an oversight. Critically, though, the paper converts importance into a binary selection via an empirical permutation-based significance threshold ("we stored the highest variable importance, repeating 1,000 times, and the α = 0.05 percentile as the threshold"), so its central conclusions (e.g., "RF model identified 44 highly important markers, with major loci on chromosomes 6A and 6D") rest on selection counts, not on fine-grained magnitude/rank ordering of importance scores. The authors also explicitly self-diagnose the very bias mechanism at issue — "unlike EN and XGB, RF lacks feature regularization resulting in correlated markers receiving similar importance scores... Investigation of these clusters revealed that most of the markers are in strong LD, thereby inflating the number of associations" — and temper their claims accordingly ("Despite these limitations...") rather than asserting the raw ranking as a load-bearing conclusion. Selected markers are further cross-checked against independent traditional GWAS tools, known literature QTLs (Juliana et al., 2019), and detailed functional/candidate-gene annotation, so even the "highly important" language is anchored to selection-overlap and biological plausibility rather than an uncorroborated impurity-magnitude ranking; SHAP is mentioned only as future work and was not actually performed. Because ranking/magnitude is not truly the load-bearing element of the stated conclusions (criterion 2 fails), the flag is refuted regardless of the corroboration gap.
